source_paper_title: Robust Sparsification for Matroid Intersection with Applications

context:
Page 6, Introduction (discussion after principal partitions / computing densest sets).

Moreover, in our algorithms, as we frequently update the ground set on which we compute the decomposition by adding or removing one element, there may be room to improve our time complexity: we leave as an open question whether updating a density-based decomposition when performing these kinds of operations can be done more efficiently, without re-computing the whole decomposition each time.
title: Efficiently update a density-based matroid decomposition under single-element modifications
background_latex:
A matroid is a pair \(M=(V,\mathcal I)\) where \(V\) is a finite ground set and \(\mathcal I\subseteq 2^V\) is a family of independent sets satisfying heredity and exchange. The rank function is \(\mathrm{rank}_M(X)=\max\{|I|: I\subseteq X,\ I\in\mathcal I\}\) for \(X\subseteq V\). For a matroid \(M\) and \(U\subseteq V\), the contraction \(M/U\) is the matroid on \(V\setminus U\) whose independent sets are \(\{S\subseteq V\setminus U: S\cup B_U\in\mathcal I\}\), where \(B_U\) is any maximum independent subset of \(U\). The restriction \(M|_{V'}\) to \(V'\subseteq V\) is the matroid on \(V'\) with independent sets \(\{I\subseteq V': I\in\mathcal I\}\).

For a nonempty \(U\subseteq V\) with \(\mathrm{rank}_M(U)>0\), define its density as
\[\rho_M(U)=\frac{|U|}{\mathrm{rank}_M(U)}.\]
(If \(U=\varnothing\), set \(\rho_M(U)=0\); if \(\mathrm{rank}_M(U)=0\) and \(U\neq\varnothing\), set \(\rho_M(U)=+\infty\).)

Fix a subset \(V'\subseteq V\) and consider the restricted matroid \(M' = M|_{V'}\) of rank \(k=\mathrm{rank}_M(V)\). A density-based decomposition of \(V'\) is obtained by the following greedy process for \(j=1,2,\dots,k\):
1) in the current matroid \(M'/(U_1\cup\cdots\cup U_{j-1})\), choose a subset \(U_j\subseteq V'\setminus (U_1\cup\cdots\cup U_{j-1})\) maximizing \(\rho\) (breaking ties by taking the maximum-cardinality maximizer);
2) contract by \(U_j\) and continue.
This produces sets \(U_1,\dots,U_k\) whose union is \(V'\) (some of the last sets may be empty). The paper notes that densest subsets can be computed in polynomial time via submodular function minimization, but recomputing the entire decomposition from scratch after each modification of \(V'\) may be expensive.

In several of the paper's constructions, the maintained set \(V'\) changes by inserting or deleting a single element \(e\in V\). The question concerns maintaining (updating) the density-based decomposition of the current \(V'\) under such single-element updates, faster than full recomputation each time (e.g., in terms of the size \(|V'|\), the matroid rank \(k\), and the cost of rank/value oracle calls).
problem_definition_latex:
Given a matroid \(M=(V,\mathcal I)\) accessed via standard matroid oracles (e.g., rank or independence), and a dynamically changing subset \(V'\subseteq V\), maintain the density-based decomposition \((U_1,\dots,U_k)\) of \(V'\) under updates of the form \(V'\leftarrow V'\cup\{e\}\) (insertion) or \(V'\leftarrow V'\setminus\{e\}\) (deletion).

Design an algorithm that updates \((U_1,\dots,U_k)\) after each single-element insertion/deletion more efficiently than recomputing the entire density-based decomposition from scratch, and characterize its update time (and oracle complexity) as a function of \(|V'|\), \(|V|\), and \(k=\mathrm{rank}_M(V)\).

location_in_paper: Page 6, Introduction (discussion after principal partitions / computing densest sets).
open_problem_quote: Moreover, in our algorithms, as we frequently update the ground set on which we compute the decomposition by adding or removing one element, there may be room to improve our time complexity: we leave as an open question whether updating a density-based decomposition when performing these kinds of operations can be done more efficiently, without re-computing the whole decomposition each time.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is an algorithm-design question rather than a universal-quantifier claim about all instances; there is no clear finite parameter space where automated search for counterexamples is meaningful.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate update procedures can be represented as code and benchmarked on randomly generated matroids with oracle implementations, but there is no canonical automatic fitness signal for worst-case (oracle) complexity, and performance may depend strongly on the matroid representation and adversarial update sequences.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
